items.objective: Ground SD-005 (z_self / z_world latent split) in biological efference-copy and self-model literature. Key question: does the brain maintain a dedicated motor-self representation (analogous to z_self) that is computationally separable from world-model representations (z_world)? Collects evidence for the z_self/z_world split architecture and the claim that combining them in a single latent (z_gamma) misattributes causal credit.
items.plain_english_claim_description: The brain keeps a separate internal model of what the body/self is doing (efference copy, proprioceptive self-model) that is distinct from its model of the external world. These two streams feed different downstream systems.
items.plain_english_ree_fit_description: REE splits z_gamma into z_self (E2 / motor-sensory domain) and z_world (E3 / hippocampus / residue domain). Biological self-model literature should ground this split in known cortical/sub-cortical circuits.

required_record_contract.summary: include caveats, disconfirming evidence, and mapping limits

research_questions: Is there direct neural evidence for separate self-model and world-model streams (e.g., parietal vs frontal dissociation)?
Does efference copy / forward model update a self-channel that does NOT update the world model (consistent with z_self isolation)?
Does MOSAIC (Wolpert & Kawato 1998) provide a computational precedent for the z_self / z_world split?
Does TPJ lesion / TMS evidence (Blanke, Farrer & Frith) support a dedicated agency-attribution region that operates on a clean world model?
What is the main disconfirming evidence — cases where self and world representations are demonstrably fused?

completion_status.notes: Entries split across mech_089 dir (2: Lisman/Jensen, Canolty/Knight) and mech_090 dir (3: beta_status_quo, Engel/Fries, Pfurtscheller/LopesDaSilva)

items.objective: Ground MECH-089 (theta-gamma nesting packages E1 updates for E3) and MECH-090 (beta oscillations gate E3 policy propagation during committed sequences) in biological oscillatory gating literature. Key question: do beta oscillations selectively suppress output propagation (not internal updating) during stable motor sequences, and does theta-gamma cross-frequency coupling package sensory/prediction error updates for higher-order evaluation?
items.plain_english_claim_description: During a committed movement sequence, beta oscillations rise and suppress the output of the planning system — not the planning itself — effectively holding the current plan stable. At sequence completion, beta drops and the updated plan propagates. Separately, theta-gamma nesting batches rapid sensory prediction updates into slower theta-cycle summaries that higher-level systems can read without being overwhelmed by moment-to-moment noise.
items.plain_english_ree_fit_description: MECH-090: beta gate elevates during committed trajectory, blocking E3 output→action; releases when uncommitted. MECH-089: E1 (fast sensorium) updates are theta-batched before E3 samples them. Both mechanisms depend on SD-006 async multi-rate execution.

required_record_contract.summary: include caveats, disconfirming evidence, and mapping limits

research_questions: Does beta power specifically suppress motor output during stable sequences, while leaving internal motor cortex computation intact?
Is there evidence for beta-gated action suppression (vs. beta-gated attention or perception)?
Does theta-gamma coupling in hippocampus or cortex batch fast updates into theta-cycle packages accessible to slower systems?
What is the main disconfirming evidence — alternative roles for beta (idle rhythm, anti-movement) that would undermine the 'output gate' account?
Do BG-thalamo-cortical loops show characteristic beta vs theta vs gamma signatures consistent with ARC-023 (separate loops at different rates)?
